meta.title: The Vanishing Buffalo: The Great Collapse, 1800–1900
meta.description: A scroll-driven visualization of the North American buffalo population collapse, with cited sources for every data point.
meta.sources_note: Population estimates are interpolated between documented historical records. Exact numbers are approximations; the scale of collapse is undisputed. Sources: Isenberg (2000), Phillips (2024), MacEwan (1995), Hornaday (1889), Flores (2016), Calloway (1996).

events.label: A Vast Ocean of Buffalo
events.text: Tens of millions of buffalo covered the plains from Alberta to Texas [1]. For the Métis, the buffalo was everything: food, clothing, shelter, trade goods. The hunt was communal, organized, and sustainable. It had been that way for generations [2].

citations.source: Flores, Dan. (2016). American Serengeti. University of Oklahoma Press.
Peterson, Jacqueline. (1985). Many Roads to Red River. Gabriel Dumont Institute.

events.alt: A herd of plains buffalo at Buffalo National Park, Wainwright, Alberta, early 1900s
events.caption: A herd of plains buffalo at Buffalo National Park, Wainwright, Alberta. The survivors of near-extinction, gathered in a protected sanctuary. (Bell Photo, Library and Archives Canada, PA-005127)

events.label: The Fur Trade Intensifies
events.text: The Hudson's Bay Company and North West Company competed for control of the plains. Métis freighters and hunters became the backbone of the trade. Pemmican — dried buffalo meat mixed with fat — fed the fur trade. The buffalo economy was booming, but the pressure was building [3].

citations.source: Isenberg, Andrew C. (2000). The Destruction of the Bison. Cambridge University Press.

events.alt: Métis freighting brigade with Red River cart and canvas lodge on the open prairie
events.caption: A Métis freighting brigade on the open prairie. The Red River cart and canvas lodge were the backbone of the fur trade economy. (Library and Archives Canada)

events.label: The Hide Trade Takes Off
events.text: Commercial hunting for buffalo robes expanded east. For every robe that made it to market, several more buffalo were killed and left in the sun [3]. The trade obscured the true death toll. By the 1870s, the robe trade peaked at 250,000 per year [3].

citations.source: Isenberg, Andrew C. (2000). The Destruction of the Bison. Cambridge University Press.

events.alt: Hunters standing over dead buffalo, Scotty Philip's herd, Fort Pierre, South Dakota
events.caption: Hunters with the carcasses of buffalo, Fort Pierre, South Dakota. (LCCN 2006689761)

events.label: Railroads Reach the Plains
events.text: Railroads made mass slaughter possible. Hunters fired from train windows, leaving carcasses to rot [3]. The bone trade shipped tons of remains east for fertilizer and sugar refining [3]. The AT&SF depot at Dodge City shipped 200,000 hides per year [3].

citations.source: Isenberg, Andrew C. (2000). The Destruction of the Bison. Cambridge University Press.

events.alt: Canadian Pacific boxcar loaded with buffalo bones on the prairie
events.caption: The railroad made mass transport of hides and bones profitable. A Canadian Pacific boxcar loaded with buffalo bones on the plains, late 1880s.

events.label: The Great Collapse Begins
events.text: The population crashed from millions to hundreds of thousands in just a few years. Métis communities faced starvation as the buffalo economy vanished [4]. In 1874, the U.S. Army openly championed killing buffalo to force Indigenous nations onto reservations. Gen. Philip Sheridan told Congress: \"Let them kill, skin, and sell until the buffalo are exterminated\" [3].

citations.source: Calloway, Colin. (1996). Our Hearts Fell to the Ground. Bedford/St. Martin's.
Isenberg, Andrew C. (2000). The Destruction of the Bison. Cambridge University Press.

events.alt: Rath & Wright's buffalo hide yard in 1878, showing 40,000 buffalo hides, Dodge City, Kansas
events.caption: Rath & Wright's buffalo hide yard, 1878, 40,000 buffalo hides, Dodge City, Kansas. The epicenter of the 1870s commercial slaughter. (Unknown photographer, public domain)

events.label: Peak Slaughter: 1871–1874
events.text: In four years, hunters killed nearly three million buffalo on the southern plains alone [5]. Carcasses stretched for miles along the railroad lines. The new 1871 tanning process turned hides into industrial belting for Eastern factories — the buffalo died for machine belts [7]. At Fort MacLeod, I.G. Baker Co. shipped 250,000 hides in a single season [5]. General Sheridan told Congress: \"Let them kill, skin, and sell until the buffalo are exterminated\" — extermination was now U.S. policy [6]. The Métis watched their economy, their food, their world vanish in one winter.

citations.source: MacEwan, Grant. (1995). Buffalo: Sacred and Sacrificed. Edmonton.
Isenberg, Andrew C. (2000). The Destruction of the Bison. Cambridge University Press.
U.S. Congress. (1874). Congressional Globe, 43rd Congress. Sheridan testimony.

events.alt: Buffalo hunt in Taylor County, 1874, from historical publication
events.caption: Buffalo Hunt in Taylor County, 1874 — peak slaughter year. Hunters working at industrial scale. (Historical publication, public domain)

events.label: The Hide Trade Collapses
events.text: The southern herd was destroyed in the early 1870s [4]. By 1877, I.G. Baker Co. marketed only 30,000 hides — down from 250,000 just three years prior [5]. The northern herd disappeared between 1876 and 1883 [4]. Treaty 7 was signed September 22, 1877; Elders recall the last buffalo vanished shortly after [4].

citations.source: MacEwan, Grant. (1995). Buffalo: Sacred and Sacrificed. Edmonton.
Calloway, Colin. (1996). Our Hearts Fell to the Ground. Bedford/St. Martin's.

events.alt: Eight-man buffalo hunt skinning a carcass, 1870s
events.caption: Buffalo hunt party skinning carcasses. LCCN 2006688689.

events.label: Picking the Bones Clean
events.text: With the herds gone, a new industry rose: bone picking [3]. Settlers and Indigenous people gathered bleached skeletons from the prairie. The CPR shipped boxcars full of bones east to Regina (the \"Pile of Bones\"), Moose Jaw, and Medicine Hat [5]. Bones became fertilizer, bone char, and buttons. Nothing was wasted except the buffalo itself [3].

citations.source: Isenberg, Andrew C. (2000). The Destruction of the Bison. Cambridge University Press.
MacEwan, Grant. (1995). Buffalo: Sacred and Sacrificed. Edmonton.

events.alt: Pile of bison skulls on the prairie, 1870s
events.caption: Gathering buffalo bones on the prairie. The skulls told the story the living no longer could.

events.label: The Northern Herd Vanishes
events.text: The northern herd disappeared between 1876 and 1883 [4]. Peak years for the buffalo bone trade were 1891–1892, when train freight cars in constant use could not keep up with demand [5]. Hundreds of thousands of skeletons piled up along railway tracks awaiting shipment [5].

citations.source: Calloway, Colin. (1996). Our Hearts Fell to the Ground. Bedford/St. Martin's.
MacEwan, Grant. (1995). Buffalo: Sacred and Sacrificed. Edmonton.

events.alt: Gull Lake, Saskatchewan, 1890 — bone trade era
events.caption: Gull Lake, Saskatchewan, 1890. Bone trade collection point on the CPR line.

events.label: The Last of the Herds
events.text: William Temple Hornaday's 1889 Smithsonian report documented the final toll: fewer than 1,000 buffalo remained in the wild [6]. The species was functionally extinct. The Métis homeland was shattered. The plains were silent [4].

citations.source: Hornaday, William Temple. (1889). The Extermination of the American Bison. Smithsonian Institution Annual Report.
Calloway, Colin. (1996). Our Hearts Fell to the Ground. Bedford/St. Martin's.

events.alt: Pile of bison skulls from the 1870s
events.caption: The skulls told the story the living no longer could.

events.label: Functionally Extinct
events.text: By 1900, roughly 500 buffalo remained, scattered in private herds and a small wild population in Yellowstone [6]. In a single lifetime, the most abundant large mammal in North America had been reduced to nearly nothing. The Métis lost their economy, their food sovereignty, and the foundation of their way of life [4]. The plains were opened for settlement. The destruction was the point [3].

citations.source: Hornaday, William Temple. (1889). The Extermination of the American Bison. Smithsonian.
Calloway, Colin. (1996). Our Hearts Fell to the Ground. Bedford/St. Martin's.
Isenberg, Andrew C. (2000). The Destruction of the Bison. Cambridge University Press.

events.alt: Stack of buffalo skulls at railway siding, Saskatchewan, ca. 1890
events.caption: Stack of buffalo skulls at a railway siding, Saskatchewan, ca. 1890. (Glenbow Museum)
events.finalAlt: Field of buffalo bones near Lloydminster, Saskatchewan, at Chief Poundmaker's last great corral, ca. 1874
events.finalCaption: Buffalo bones near Lloydminster, Saskatchewan — Chief Poundmaker's last great corral, ca. 1874. The sea of bone stretches to the horizon. (Ernest Brown, copyright)
events.closingText: Conservation efforts in the early 1900s brought the species back from the brink. Today, roughly 500,000 buffalo exist. Most are in commercial herds. A few wild, free-roaming populations remain.

sources.full: Flores, Dan. (2016). American Serengeti. University of Oklahoma Press.
Peterson, Jacqueline. (1985). Many Roads to Red River. Gabriel Dumont Institute.
Isenberg, Andrew C. (2000). The Destruction of the Bison: An Environmental History, 1750–1920. Cambridge University Press.
Calloway, Colin. (1996). Our Hearts Fell to the Ground: Plains Indian Views of How the West Was Lost. Bedford/St. Martin's.
MacEwan, Grant. (1995). Buffalo: Sacred and Sacrificed. Edmonton: Alberta Sport, Recreation, Parks and Wildlife Foundation.
Hornaday, William Temple. (1889). "The Extermination of the American Bison." Smithsonian Institution Annual Report.
Taylor, Scott. (2011). "Buffalo Hunt: International Trade and the Virtual Extinction of the North American Bison." American Economic Review 101, no. 7: 3162–3195.
Doback, William. (2006). "Killing the Canadian Buffalo, 1821–1881." In Canadian Environmental History: Essential Readings, ed. David Freeland Duke. Toronto: Canadian Scholars' Press.
Geist, Valerius. (1996). Buffalo Nation: History and Legend of the North American Bison. Stillwater: Voyageur Press.
Smits, David. (1994). "The Frontier Army and the Destruction of the Buffalo: 1865–1883." Western Historical Quarterly 25, no. 3: 312–338.
U.S. Congress. (1874). Congressional Globe, 43rd Congress. Gen. Philip Sheridan testimony on buffalo destruction policy.
Phillips, Nancy. (2024). "Skin and Bones: The Decimation of the Plains Buffalo." Mount Royal University Humanities Review, Vol. 5.
